below the level of the carina at the cavoatrial junction.  Pulmonary 
edema is again demonstrated.

tube with persistent right apical pneumothorax.  The
cardiomediastinal silhouette is unremarkable.  There is tenting of
the right hemidiaphragm, secondary to upper lobe volume loss from
surgery.  The left lung is clear.  A small amount of subcutaneous
emphysema is seen in the neck.  Partial resection of a right-sided
rib is again noted.
 Follow up study again demonstrates the right apical pneumothorax
without other significant change.

with its tip in the proximal stomach and its distal side port likely 
in the distal esophagus.  There is also a new right internal jugular 
central venous catheter.  There are persistent diffuse reticular 
opacifications, slightly worse when compared to the prior film, 
representing pulmonary edema.  There is a stable left pleural 
effusion.

demonstrates slightly improved pulmonary edema and interval 
intubation with the endotracheal tube located approximately 3.2 cm 
above the carina.  No other significant changes are noted.
 
A third and final chest x-ray from 12-7-16 at 427 and image is 
further improvement of reticular opacification bilaterally compatible 
with improving edema.  The nasogastric tube has been advanced further 
into the stomach, its tip is not visualized.

section_findings:
A single semi-erect AP view of the chest on 4/11/2000 at 1606 hours
demonstrates interval placement of a right chest tube with
development of a hydropneumothorax. The right apex demonstrates
pleural thickening versus located effusion. The underlying right
lung demonstrates patchy opacification at the base which may
represent atelectasis versus postoperative change. The left lung
appears clear with minimal blunting of the costophrenic sulcus
which may represent pleural thickening versus effusion. Cardiac
size is within normal limits. Tortuosity of the descending
thoracic aorta.
Follow-up exam at 1701 hours demonstrates interval decrease in size
of the right-sided pneumothorax. Otherwise, no significant
interval change.

permanent pacemaker in place. Median sternotomy wires appear intact, 
with a prosthetic cardiac valve and unchanged cardiomegaly. There is 
mild prominence of the central pulmonary vasculature, and a few 
septal lines are seen at the right base, but could reflect early or 
resolving interstitial pulmonary edema. No evidence of pleural 
effusion. The lungs otherwise appear clear.

the left, and surgical clips are seen in the right axilla.
 
Mild dextroscoliosis of the lower thoracic spine, with multilevel 
level degenerative changes.

small left pneumothorax seen on CT examination is not visualized 
likely due to supine technique.  There has been interval insertion of 
a left chest tube with the tip projecting over the left mid lung. 
There are low lung volumes.  Increasing consolidation is noted in the 
left upper and middle lobes, compatible with pulmonary contusion. 
Known small right pulmonary contusions are not seen. The 
cardiomediastinal silhouette is stable.  Bilateral rib fractures are 
again seen.

section_findings:
Interval change shows placement of a right internal jugular central
venous line with the tip in the superior portion of the superior
vena cava. There is also an endotracheal tube with tip
approximately 4 cm above the level of the carina. There is a
pericardial drain, as well as epicardial pacer wires. No change in
orthopedic hardware in the mid to lower portion of the thoracic
spine. There is an abnormal left retrocardiac air opacity,
worrisome for consolidation versus atelectasis. There are probable
small pleural effusions. There is also a small right probable
pneumothorax.
Subsequent film on 9/30/2016 shows slight advancement of the
endotracheal tube, now approximately 2 cm above the level of the
carina. There is also interval placement of a nasogastric tube
with tip in the subdiaphragmatic position. There is increasing
bilateral pleural effusions. The pneumothorax on the right persist
and is small, unchanged. There is persistent left retrocardiac
air opacity.

CHEST:  5-28-2003 
 
 COMPARISON:  None. 
 
 CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 65-year-old man with a history of 
hypersensitivity and pneumonitis, now with right lateral rib pain at 
the level of the nipple.  Chest x-ray to rule out pneumothorax. 
 
 FINDINGS:  Frontal and lateral views of the chest taken on 
5/28/2003 demonstrate normal cardiomediastinal silhouette.  Fine 
reticular opacities are seen in the lung with peripheral and basilar 
predominance.  They are particularly prominent on the lateral view at 
the posterior lungs.  Distribution is suggestive of pulmonary 
fibrosis.  Additional nodular patterns can be seen at the peripheral 
upper lungs.  While they most likely represent additional area of 
fibrosis, active pneumonitis cannot absolutely exclude it.  
Costophrenic sulci are sharp.  There is no evidence for pneumothorax.  
The ribs, where visualized, show no evidence of displaced fracture.  
There is evidence for osteopenia.

section_findings:
The previously seen left loculated pneumothorax is no
longer visualized. There is a small to moderate left pleural
effusion with probable associated left basilar atelectasis. The
retrocardiac opacity is unchanged and again may represent
postsurgical scarring versus atelectasis. Superimposed infection
cannot be excluded. The emphysematous right lung is unchanged,
including two nodular opacities in the mid lung which were present
on prior chest CT 24/07. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is
unchanged, including left hilar surgical clips. Bones are
unremarkable.

cardiomegaly.  There is opacity in the right lung base which likely 
represents a combination of elevation of the right hemidiaphragm as 
well as atelectasis.  On the lateral view, there is a spherical 
lucency, which on the AP view appears in or adjacent to the right 
minor fissure, which may represent a bleb and/or herniation.  There 
is faint indistinctness to the vascularity, and mild peribronchial 
cuffing which may relate to pulmonary edema.  The bony structures are 
normal.

AP semierect view of the chest demonstrates worsening 
bilateral alveolar pulmonary edema.  Retrocardiac consolidation and a 
left pleural effusion persists unchanged.  Left PICC line is stable 
and satisfactory in position

section_impression:

REDEMONSTRATION OF HIATAL HERNIA.  POSSIBLE SMALL LEFT PLEURAL 
EFFUSION.  STABLE CARDIAC SILHOUETTE.

section_impression:
1. SINGLE AP VIEW OF THE CHEST DEMONSTRATES STABLE POSITIONING OF
SUPPORTING LINES AND TUBES.
2. STABLE COMPLETE OPACIFICATION OF THE LEFT HEMITHORAX.
3. REDEMONSTRATION OF A PROBABLE SMALL RIGHT PLEURAL EFFUSION.
THERE IS RETICULAR PATTERN OF THE RIGHT LUNG, LIKELY DUE TO
PULMONARY EDEMA, AS WELL AS INCREASED HYPERVASCULARITY OF THE RIGHT
LUNG. THERE IS INCREASED AIR SPACE OPACITY IN THE MEDIAL RIGHT
LUNG BASE FOR WHICH SUPERIMPOSED INFECTION OR ASPIRATION CANNOT BE
EXCLUDED.
4. REDEMONSTRATION OF DIFFUSELY INCREASED ATTENUATION OF THE
BONES, CONSISTENT WITH DIFFUSE BONY SCLEROTIC METASTATIC DISEASE.

recovery room demonstrates a left chest tube in place. The end of
the chest tube is bent at the apex with the tip directed toward the

are multiple linear densities projecting over the upper chest,
likely external to the patient related to the sheets. This limits

small left apical pneumothorax present. The lungs are otherwise
clear with no pleural effusions or pulmonary edema. The
cardiomediastinal silhouette is unremarkable. The skeletal
structures are grossly unremarkable.

internal jugular catheter and pacer/AICD device overlying the left
hemithorax with a lead in the RA and a lead in the RV. These are

section_findings:
AP portable view of the chest taken on 19/06 VasoPrep Surgical 0820 hours, 
demonstrates a right-sided chest tube, with its tip at the apex of 
the right hemithorax.  No definite right-sided pneumothorax is seen.  
There is persistent elevation of the right hemidiaphragm.  A subtle, 
2 cm nodular opacity is seen projecting over the right clavicle and 
the posterior right 4th rib.  This appears unchanged since prior 
study.  Left lung is clear.  Both costophrenic sulci are sharp.  
There is subcutaneous emphysema across the right check and the right 
neck base. 
 
 AP portable view of the chest taken on 2-19-2006 VasoPrep Surgical 0938 hours, 
demonstrates a right-sided chest tube, position unchanged.  There is 
no evidence for pneumothorax.  No other significant interval change.

section_findings:
Tunneled right internal jugular dual-lumen hemodialysis 
catheter with distal tip at the cavoatrial junction.  There is 
cardiomegaly and interstitial vascular prominence compatible with 
pulmonary edema.  There is silhouetting of the right heart border 
compatible with a right middle lobe consolidation.  There is also 
opacity at the more lateral right lung base and blunting of the right 
costophrenic angle suggestive of a small pleural effusion.    No 
discrete osseous abnormality.  Visualization of the vertebral bodies 
is limited.
section_clinical_history:
Seventy-five-year-old female with renal failure on 
hemodialysis and one day of productive cough.

Frontal and lateral views of the chest demonstrate a nodular opacity 
within the peripheral left lung base with chronic minimal blunting of 
the left costophrenic angle, as well as partial obscuration of the 
left hemidiaphragm.  The lungs are otherwise clear.  Stable 
postsurgical changes within the mediastinum.  The cardiomediastinal 
silhouette and pulmonary vasculature are stable in configuration.  
Visualized osseous structures demonstrate no acute abnormalities.

section_findings:
AP portable chest November 22nd, 2017 compared with 11/22/2017:  Lines
and tubes stable and satisfactory.  Near complete opacification of
the right hemithorax persists with some minimal aeration of the right
hemithorax apex which may be in the pleural cavity.  Postop change
right lateral chest wall consistent with prior pneumonectomy again
noted.  No definite aerated lung identified on the right, without
change in appearance since 11/22/2017.
Improved aeration of the left lung base with decreased atelectasis at
the retrocardiac area.   However, there is still persistent, faint,
reticular, nodular opacity throughout the left lung of uncertain
cause.  This could be due to underlying disease, or interstitial
edema which persists.

section_impression:
1. There has been interval placement of an ET tube with the tip
located approximately 2.5 cm above the carina, as well as an NG
tube in satisfactory position. There is redemonstration of a right
PICC line, which is unchanged.
2. There are persistent low lung volumes, worsening bilateral
opacities with bilateral pleural effusions, and evidence of
worsening pulmonary edema.
3. Follow up study demonstrates interval placement of a IJ with
the tip at the cavoatrial junction, and persistent pulmonary edema.
4. Follow up study demonstrates interval removal of the right PICC
line, with no other significant changes.

section_findings:
Single view of the chest obtained postoperatively, 8-28-2001 at 1750
(592186862913436) demonstrates interval placement of two right internal
jugular central venous catheters, endotracheal tube, and
nasogastric tube. A mediastinal drain is seen. There is prominence
of the mediastinum, likely postoperative. There has been interval
placement of median sternotomy wires and vascular clips. There is
mild pulmonary edema. A loculated left sided pleural effusion and
left base opacity are seen.
Repeat chest x-ray 8/28/01 at 2030 (592186862913436  ) demonstrates interval
increase in size of the left sided pleural effusion with decreased
aeration of the left lung.
Repeat chest x-ray 8/28/2001 at 2226 (592186862913436) demonstrates interval
placement of a left sided chest tube with decreased size of the
left sided pleural effusion.
Chest x-ray 8/28/01 at 0351 (RV-FH-AH-ZK-H) shows interval decrease in
lung volumes compared with the prior examination but otherwise no
change.

section_findings:
Upright PA and lateral chest radiographs demonstrate widening of
the superior mediastinum, measuring 12 cm in maximal dimension.
There is a prominent soft tissue convexity in the expected region
of the azygous node. There is also pleural thickening along the
right apex medially, just adjacent to the trachea, raising the
question of underlying peritracheal lymphadenopathy. There is
increased density within the retrosternal region on the lateral
view suggestive of an anterior mediastinal process. The overall
cardiomediastinal silhouette takes a somewhat triangular-shape,
raising the question of an underlying pericardial effusion. The
lungs are clear bilaterally. There is minimal blunting of the
right lateral costophrenic sulcus which may be related to either
pleural thickening or a very small pleural effusion. No evidence
of left pleural effusion. No pulmonary edema. The skeletal
structures are grossly unremarkable.

change.

patient significant rotated to the right.
There are diffuse air space opacities involving both lung bases,
left greater than right. These findings are worrisome for a
multifocal pneumonia. Other possibilities would be alveolar edema
or aspiration. Correlate clinically.

patient positioning, however, cannot exclude a right apical process
and/or mass. Would recommend a repeat PA and lateral chest x-ray
when the patient can tolerate.
